title: Buy vs Build vs Fine-tune: Check your understanding

questions.question: A team wants their customer service AI to always respond in a specific conversational tone and always follow a particular response structure: concise acknowledgement, solution, next step. They find the model is inconsistent. They are considering fine-tuning. Is this the right decision?
options: Yes: tone and structure consistency is exactly what fine-tuning solves

Possibly, but only after exhausting prompt engineering first: tone, structure, and format are behaviour gaps that good prompting with few-shot examples often resolves without the data labelling overhead of fine-tuning
No: tone cannot be changed through fine-tuning; it is a model property
Yes, but only if they have at least 10,000 labelled examples
questions.explanation: Tone and response format are classic behaviour gaps, and they are exactly the kind of thing that well-crafted system prompts and a handful of few-shot examples can address. Fine-tuning is warranted when prompting genuinely cannot close the gap, not as a first resort. The cost of not testing prompting first is substantial: data labelling, training compute, model hosting, and ongoing retraining. Exhaust prompting before committing to that overhead.

questions.question: An organisation has a large internal knowledge base, product documentation, policy documents, historical case records, and wants the AI to answer questions using this information accurately. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Fine-tuning on the knowledge base documents so the model memorises the content
Retrieval-Augmented Generation: retrieve relevant documents at query time and include them in the prompt, so the model answers from current documents rather than memorised content
Buying a commercial AI product and uploading the documents to the vendor
Fine-tuning on question/answer pairs derived from the knowledge base
questions.explanation: A knowledge gap (the model doesn't know your internal facts) is solved by retrieval, not fine-tuning. Fine-tuning on documents trains the model to recall facts at inference time, but facts change, product specs update, policies evolve, cases close, and you cannot retrain every time they do. RAG retrieves the current document at query time, so the AI always answers from up-to-date information. Fine-tuning on knowledge base content is expensive to maintain and produces a model that is out-of-date as soon as anything changes.

questions.question: A company signs an enterprise contract with an AI vendor whose proprietary API is central to their application. Six months later the vendor raises prices by 50%. What risk did this outcome represent, and what mitigation should have been in place?
options: Strategic risk; the mitigation is to negotiate a lower price
Vendor lock-in risk; the mitigation is to abstract the vendor behind an internal interface layer from the start, maintaining the ability to switch providers without rewriting the entire application
Operational risk; the mitigation is an SLA with financial penalties
Model risk; the mitigation is to evaluate multiple vendors before signing
questions.explanation: This is a vendor lock-in risk. When the vendor's proprietary API is directly embedded throughout the application, switching requires rewriting the application: which creates leverage for the vendor to raise prices. The mitigation is architectural: build an abstraction layer that wraps the vendor-specific code and presents a stable internal interface. Switching then means updating the abstraction layer, not the entire application. This should be built in from the start, because retrofitting it after lock-in is exactly as expensive as it sounds.

questions.question: A team fine-tunes a model on 2,000 examples of their desired output style and behaviour. After deployment, the model is more consistent, but the outputs are worse than before. What most likely went wrong?
options: 2,000 examples is not enough; they need at least 20,000
The training examples contained the bad behaviour they were trying to fix, or inconsistent quality: fine-tuning made the bad behaviour more consistent, not better
The fine-tuned model was deployed on an incompatible infrastructure
Fine-tuning always degrades model quality in the first iteration
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning amplifies the signal in your training data. If the training examples contain the problem you were trying to solve, wrong tone, incorrect format, bad reasoning patterns, the fine-tuned model learns to replicate that problem more reliably. 'More consistent' after fine-tuning on bad examples means consistently bad. Data quality review before labelling, clear annotation guidelines, inter-annotator agreement checks, and a holdout eval set to measure whether fine-tuning actually improved things are all essential prerequisites.

questions.question: Which combination of approaches best addresses a use case where the AI needs to behave in a very specific domain-adapted way AND needs access to continuously updated information?
options: Buy a commercial product and configure it
Fine-tune for behaviour (teaching the model how to respond in your context) combined with RAG for knowledge (retrieving current facts at query time)
Fine-tune on both the behaviour examples and the knowledge documents
Build using prompt engineering only, with no retrieval or fine-tuning
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning and RAG address complementary gaps. Fine-tuning teaches the model how to behave, the tone, format, decision patterns, and domain conventions specific to your use case. RAG provides what the model needs to know at any given moment, current documents, live records, up-to-date facts. Combining both gives you domain-appropriate behaviour plus current knowledge, without the need to retrain every time facts change. This hybrid approach is the standard pattern for mature domain-specific AI applications.
